generations.text:
My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with trembling hands, for the wind howls like a banshee and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very earth. The lighthouse stands as it always has, a solitary sentinel against the chaos, but I fear it may not stand much longer. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet the weight of silence presses upon me as heavily as the storm that now gathers.

You were never meant to leave, Eliza. Not then, not ever. I had hoped the sea would carry your regrets away, but it only brings them back with each wave. I have not forgotten the day you left, nor the way you turned your back on this place, on me. You were but a child then, and I a man who had long since lost the will to be anything more than a keeper of lights and keeper of memories.

I have kept the lighthouse these many years, not for the glory of it, nor for the wages, but because it is all I have left. The world moves on, as it always has, and I have been left behind, like the tide that lingers too long on the shore. I have seen the stars, and the moon, and the dawn that follows even the darkest night. I have seen the passing of seasons, and the rise and fall of the waves, and I have seen my own heart grow colder with each passing year.

Tonight, however, is different.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the sea howling at my window, the wind clawing at the shutters like a beast determined to tear down the walls of this old lighthouse. The sky is a bruise of black and silver, and the waves crash against the rocks below with a fury that makes my bones ache. I’ve spent most of the night huddled in the lantern room, the light flickering like a dying breath, and I find myself thinking of you—of the last time we spoke, of the silence that followed, and of the things I never said.

Five years, Eleanor. Five years since you left. I’ve tried to forget, but the sea has a way of keeping things close. It carries them in the tide, in the whisper of the waves, in the way the wind moans like a mother’s lament. I’ve spent every night since you left staring into the abyss of the ocean, wondering if you ever look out at it from your own window, wondering if you ever think of me at all.

I know I’ve been a failure as a father. I know I’ve let you down. I should have fought harder to keep you here, to keep us together. I should have understood that the world you wanted was not the world I could give you. I was too stubborn, too set in my ways, too afraid to change. I thought I was protecting you, but I was only pushing you away.

I

**April 12th, 1893**

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with the sea howling at my window, the wind clawing at the shutters like some damned thing with a grudge. The lantern burns low, and I fear the light may be the last thing I shall ever see. But I must write, for if I do not, I fear I shall be remembered only by the sea, and not by you.

Five years have passed since you left this house, and I have not heard a word from you. I have not seen your face, nor heard your voice, nor felt the weight of your presence in this old stone tower. I have tried, Eleanor. I have tried to forgive, to forget, to carry on. But the sea does not forgive, and neither does the heart.

You were a child when you ran away, and I was a man who did not know how to hold you. I thought I was protecting you, that the world was too cruel, too cold, too full of men who would take advantage of a girl with no father and no fortune. But I was wrong. I was afraid. And I let fear guide me, not love.

I have watched the tides rise and fall, the stars blink out one by one, and the storms come and go. Each one a reminder of the distance between us. Each one a whisper that I may not be able to reach you.

I have thought

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you, though I fear it may find you in a place where your heart is more broken than your body. Five years have passed since I last laid eyes upon you, and though time has a way of dulling the edges of pain, it has not dulled the ache of your absence.

You were always a fire, Eliza—bright, uncontainable, and far too quick to burn out. I have spent these years watching from the shore, hoping for a sign, a letter, a whisper of your voice, but all I have heard is the howl of the wind and the echo of my own regrets.

Tonight, the storm is coming. Not the kind that merely shakes the lighthouse, but the kind that may shake the very bones of this old world. The sky is black as a sailor’s soul, and the sea roars like a beast awakened from a century of slumber. I have lit the beacon, as I have done for every storm since the day you left me. But tonight, I fear I may not be able to keep it lit much longer.

You were not the first to leave me, Eliza. I had the sea, the wind, the darkness—each of them faithful in their own way. But you, my daughter, you were the only one who ever truly understood me. You saw through the grime of my hands and the shadows in my eyes. You knew

**[Sealed with a wax crest of a lighthouse and a single tear-streaked rose]**

**To My Dearest Elara,**

**My Dearest Elara,**

It is with a heart heavy with sorrow and a soul aching with longing that I write these words to you. The ink upon this page may not capture the depth of my feelings, nor can the wind that howls through the rocks of this desolate coast convey the tempest of my spirit. But I must write, for the storm is coming, and I fear I may not live to see dawn.

Five years have passed since you left, Elara. Five years of silence, of whispered prayers to the saints, of nights spent staring at the sea, wondering if you ever thought of me. I have not spoken to you, not once, and yet your absence haunts me more than any ghost that walks these shores.

Tonight, the sky is dark as a widow’s shroud, and the wind howls like a beast in pain. The sea, that ever-faithful companion, has grown wild, her waves crashing against the rocks with a fury I have never seen. I fear this storm may be the end of me. I have been a lighthouse keeper for many years, but never have I felt so alone. The light is still burning, as it always has, but I am afraid it may soon be the only thing left to guide the lost and the damned.

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

**My Dearest Eliza,**

It has been five long years since we last spoke, and I find myself writing this letter with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast through the cracks of this old lighthouse. The storm is upon us, and I fear the sea may take more than just my lamp tonight. But I must write, for I know you are not here to hear these words, and I must not let the silence between us grow any longer.

You were a spirited girl, Eliza, with a mind as sharp as the sea's edge and a heart that beat with the rhythm of the tides. I remember the day you left—your eyes full of fire and your words sharp as the cutlass you carried. You called me a "stuffy, old sea dog" and said you could not bear to live in the shadow of a man who had never known what it was to truly live. I was hurt, of course, but I was also proud. You were always the bold one, the one who sought the world beyond the rocks and the cliffs.

I have not seen you since that day, and I have not heard from you. I suppose you have found your way to the city, where the streets are paved with opportunity and the air is thick with the scent of ambition. I imagine you are now a proper lady, with a fine wardrobe and a husband who would never dream of a man

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I know it may not. I have written it many times, but never sent it. Tonight, the wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea roars as if it would swallow the world whole. I have lit the lamp, as I have done every night for the past five years, and for the first time, I wonder if it will ever be extinguished.

You were twelve when you left. I remember the look in your eyes—full of anger, but also of something deeper, something like sorrow. You told me you couldn’t stay, that you wanted more than this life of salt and stone and silence. I didn’t stop you. I couldn’t. I told you I loved you, and I meant it, even as my heart ached with the weight of your absence.

Five years. That’s how long it’s been since you walked out of this door. Five years since I last heard your voice. Five years since I stopped expecting you to come back. But tonight, something has changed. The storm is coming, and I fear it may take me before I can see you again.

I have no regrets, Eliza. I have done what I thought was right. I have kept this lighthouse, I have watched over the sea, I have held the light for all who sail these waters. But I have never held the light for you. And I fear

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write to you now with a heart heavy as the storm that gathers on the horizon, and with hands that tremble not from the wind, but from the weight of years unspoken. Five long years have passed since we last exchanged a word, and in that time, the sea has whispered secrets I have dared not share.

You were young when you left, full of fire and fury, and I, in my stubborn pride, allowed you to go. I told myself I was protecting you, that the world was too cruel, too vast, and that the sea was too wild for a woman of your spirit. But I was wrong, as I have been so often. I have not been the father you deserved, nor the man you once believed in.

Tonight, the skies darken with a fury I have not seen in decades. The wind howls like a banshee, and the waves rise like giants, crashing against the cliffs with a rage that mirrors the turmoil within me. I do not know if I will see dawn. And if I do, I wonder if you will still be here to see it.

I have no regrets, only the ache of what might have been. I have watched the beacon burn through countless nights, guiding ships safely home, yet I have not been able to guide you back to me. I have kept the light alive, but my heart has long since gone dark.

If you are reading this, know that I have never stopped

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy as the stone that guards the lighthouse’s entrance. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea, that old adversary, roars with fury. I fear I may not see the dawn, and if I do not, I hope you will read this letter and know that I have not forgotten you, nor have I ceased to love you.

Five years have passed since you left this place, and five years have not been enough to dull the ache of your absence. I have kept the lighthouse, as I always have, though the light that once guided so many now seems dim in comparison to the light you once brought into my life.

You were my greatest joy, my pride, and my sorrow. I remember the day you came, a little girl with eyes like the sea and a laugh that echoed through these stones. You taught me the stars, and I taught you the ways of the sea. We were a family, and I believed that no storm could ever tear us apart. But then you left, and with you, a part of my soul.

I have heard from the village that you have taken up with a man of questionable character, a sailor who speaks of distant lands and promises of adventure. I do not know if you are better off or worse, but I cannot bring myself to wish you ill. I have always been a man of duty, of discipline, and

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart that has carried the weight of silence for far too long. The storm outside howls like a beast in pain, and I find myself wondering if it might be the same as the storm within me—unrelenting, unyielding, and deaf to the cries of those who seek its mercy.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I have spent those years in the solitude of this lighthouse, watching the sea with a gaze that has long since lost its clarity. I have been a lighthouse keeper for most of my life, and the light I guard is not just the beacon that guides ships through the night, but the light of hope, of purpose, of something greater than myself. Yet, I have never felt the light of your presence in the same way.

I have always known you were my greatest regret. I was a man who failed to be the father you needed, and I have carried that failure like a stone upon my chest. I was too busy with the duties of this post, too wrapped up in the responsibility of keeping the light burning, to be the man you deserved. I was a man who thought that distance was the only way to protect you from the pain of my shortcomings.

But now, as the storm approaches, I find myself questioning everything. I have always believed that the sea is a cruel mistress, but I have never considered that I might be the one who

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

**April 12th, 1893**

**Lighthouse Keeper’s Quarters, Point Galloway**

**Devonshire, England**

I write to you now not with the hope of rekindling what has long since died between us, but with the certainty that my time is near. The storm that brews on the horizon is not merely a tempest of wind and rain—it is a reckoning. I have seen the sky darken with a fury I have not known since my wife passed, and I fear that the sea, in its wrath, may take me before the dawn.

Five years have passed since you left our home. Five years of silence, of letters left unanswered, of visits that ended in tears and accusations. I have tried to forgive you, Eleanor. I have tried to believe that the pain of your departure was not my fault, that the cruel words you spoke were not born of a heart hardened by neglect. But I have not been able to forget. I have not been able to let you go.

You were a child once, Eleanor. You were not the woman you have become. I see that now, in the distance, in the flickering light of the lighthouse that I have tended with unwavering devotion. You were once my pride, my joy, my hope. And then you were gone, like the tide that recedes without warning.

I have not spoken of the storm to

**April 12th, 1893**

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the windowpanes. The sea, as if in mourning, rages with a fury I've never known. The lighthouse stands as it always has—sturdy, steadfast, and alone. But I am not the same man who once stood here with you, my child, on the cold stone steps, watching the waves crash against the cliffs.

Five long years have passed since you left this place. Five years since you turned your back on me, on this lighthouse, on everything that was once ours. I have not spoken to you, not even in the silence of the night, when the world is still and the only sound is the whisper of the sea. But tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself unable to remain silent.

I have always been a man of words, Eliza. A man of duty, of light, of watching over the dark. But words have failed me when it comes to you. I have tried, I have tried so hard, to find the right words to mend the rift between us. But the sea has always been louder, and the wind has always been crueler than my tongue.

You were a child when you left, no older than ten. I remember the day you ran from the house, your small hands clutching a bundle

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavier than the storm that now rages beyond these walls. Five long years have passed since I last set foot in the village, and five years since I last saw your face. I pray this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not find you at all.

The wind howls like a banshee, and the sea, that old adversary, roars with a fury I have not heard since the day I first took the helm of this lighthouse. I have spent countless nights watching the waves crash against the rocks, but never have they seemed so wild, so unrelenting. I do not know if I shall survive the night, and if I do, I wonder if I shall have the strength to write this.

You were always a child of the sea, Eleanor, and I once thought that perhaps you would one day return to this place, to this life. But you chose the city, the life of a lady, and I chose the solitude of this tower. We were never meant to part, but we did, and I have never found the words to call you back.

I have heard nothing from you, not a letter, not a single word. I have sent your father's name into the winds, but it seems the sea has taken your voice as well. I have no regrets, though I do not know if that is the truth or merely the lie I tell myself to keep from

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside this old tower, as though the very sea is crying out in protest. The storm is coming—dark and furious, and I fear it may take more than the light from this lantern. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet, tonight, the silence feels heavier than ever.

You were always a child of the sea, I suppose. I remember the way you would run along the shore, your small hands clutching at the sand as if it were a treasure. You used to ask me why the waves always seemed to be calling out to something unseen. I told you they were whispering to the lighthouse, to the light that guides the lost. But now, as I stand alone in this cold, creaking tower, I wonder if I have been the one who was lost all along.

I have not written to you, not because I do not care, but because I have been afraid. Afraid that the words would not be enough, that they would fall short of the chasm that has grown between us. I know the things I have done, the things I have failed to say. I have been a man of habit, of duty, of routine, and in doing so, I have become a stranger to the one person who once meant the world to me.

You were the light in my life, Eleanor. I used to think that

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee outside, the sea roiling as though it seeks to swallow the very rocks that have stood guard over these shores for decades. The lantern flickers, and I fear it may not hold through the night. But I must write, for if I do not, I fear I shall be left with only the silence of the storm and the weight of my regrets.

Five years have passed since you last called me *father*, and five years have felt like a lifetime. I have watched the hours pass, and with them, the last of my patience. You were a child when you left, and I was a man who believed I could keep you safe. But you chose your own path, and I chose mine. And now, as the sky darkens and the sea growls in its fury, I find myself questioning whether I have made the right choice.

I have not written to you, nor have I spoken of you, for fear that the words might not be strong enough to carry the weight of my heart. But tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I find that I must try. I must try to tell you the truth—of the man I have become, and the father I have failed to be.

You were my light, Eleanor. The soft glow of your laughter, the way you would run up the stairs to greet me with your father’s coat wrapped around your

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write to you now with trembling hands and a heart that aches like the wind howling through this lighthouse. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and though the sea has not forgotten its own, I fear I have forgotten how to speak to you.

You were always the cleverest child, Eliza—bright as the stars that light our path, sharp as the edge of a blade. But you grew up, and with you, the fire of our bond dimmed. You left, as you always did, with words and without a backward glance. I have not seen you in years, and I have not heard your voice since the last time the waves crashed like thunder against these rocks.

But tonight is different, my darling. Tonight, the storm comes with a fury I have never known. The sky is black as ink, and the wind howls like a wounded beast. I can feel the sea rising, and I fear it may take us all before dawn.

I have always been the keeper of this light, Eliza. I have watched over the waters, guided the ships, and stood alone in the cold, knowing that if I falter, others may perish. But tonight, I feel the weight of something far greater than the sea. It is the weight of regret, of longing, of a father who has not been there for his child.

You were right to leave, I think. You were right to chase

**[Sealed with a wax stamp of a lighthouse and a single tear, with the words "For My Beloved Eleanor" in faded ink.]**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

It is with a heart heavy with sorrow and a soul burdened by the weight of silence that I write to you. Five long years have passed since the last letter you sent me, and in that time, I have lived alone with the sea, the wind, and the memories of our past. Tonight, the storm is coming—a tempest of such fury that I fear it may claim the lighthouse itself, and with it, my life. I write to you now, not only in hope of finding solace in your words, but in the desperate hope that you might hear from me once more.

I have not spoken to you in five years, and I know that you have not spoken to me. I know that the silence between us has grown like the sea, vast, unyielding, and indifferent to the pain it causes. Yet I write, not to beg for your forgiveness, for I know that such words are not mine to utter, but to remind you that I have never forgotten you. You were once my light, Eleanor, and though I have not seen your face in years, I still feel your presence in every wave that crashes against the rocks, in every gust of wind that howls through the tower, and in every star

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**From Your Father, The Keeper of the Beacon at Halkyn Point**

**April 2nd, 1896**

**I write this with trembling hands, the ink barely dry upon the page, and the wind howling like a banshee outside the lighthouse. The storm is upon us, and I fear for my life. But more than that, I fear for you, my daughter, and the silence that has stretched between us like the miles between Halkyn Point and the city where you now live.**

**Five years have passed since the last time I saw your face, and I have not heard a word from you, not even a letter. You were always a spirited child, Eleanor, full of fire and ambition. I remember the day you left, the way you flung your bonnet at my chest and told me I was a "stuffy, old sea dog" who had no idea what life was really about. You were right, of course. I was a fool to think I could keep you here, bound to this lonely rock, watching the waves and the stars. But I thought I was doing what was best for you.**

**You see, my dear, I was raised to believe that duty and sacrifice were the highest virtues. I was the son of a shipwright, a man who taught me that the sea is both a friend and a foe, and that the lighthouse is

**My Dearest Clara,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I know it will not. I write with trembling hands, the wind howling like a beast outside these stone walls, and the sea roiling as if it too has heard of your name.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years of silence. Five years of watching the waves crash against the cliffs, wondering if they might one day carry your voice to me. I have tried to forget, but the sea does not forget. It carries every whisper, every sorrow, every unspoken word.

I have been alone here for so long, Clara. Not just in body, but in spirit. The lighthouse is a lonely place, and the nights are the longest. I have no one to speak to, no one to share the weight of the world with. But tonight... tonight is different. Tonight, the storm is coming, and I fear it may take more than the light.

I have not seen you in five years, and I do not know if I ever will. But I have not stopped thinking of you. I have not stopped loving you. I have not stopped hoping that one day, you will come back—not as a child, not as a daughter, but as the woman you have become. I hope you have found peace, and that you have found someone who will love you as I once did.

I know I have failed you. I know I have let you down. But

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write to you now with a heart heavy as the storm that looms on the horizon, and with hands that tremble not only from the cold but from the weight of years unspoken. Five long years have passed since we last shared a word, and in that time, the sea has whispered more to me than any letter could have ever carried.

You were young when you left, and I, in my stubbornness, let pride keep me from reaching out. I told myself that silence was a shield, that distance was a lesson. But I have come to see that it was only a wall, one that has grown taller with each passing tide.

Tonight, the sky is dark as a sailor's soul, and the wind howls like a ghost from the past. The waves rise like the memories I've tried to drown, and I find myself wondering if the sea will claim me before I can find the words to mend what was broken.

I have not forgotten you, Eleanor. Not in the least. I have watched the stars for you, and lit the beacon for you, even when you were not here. I have carried the weight of your absence, and in doing so, I have come to understand that love is not always loud, nor is it always clear.

If you are reading this, know that I am here, as I have always been. I am the keeper of this lighthouse, and I am the keeper of your heart. I

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

**April 12th, 1893**

I write to you from the solitude of the cliffs, where the wind howls like the devil himself and the sea roars with a fury that would make even the most hardened sailor tremble. The storm is upon us, and I fear it may claim me before dawn. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet, as I pen these words, I find myself compelled to do so—though I know not if it is duty or regret that moves me.

You were always a spirited child, Eleanor. I remember the day you first stood upon the cliffs, your tiny hand gripping mine as the waves crashed against the rocks below. You had the fire of a thousand suns in your eyes, and I thought then that you would grow into a woman of strength and purpose. But you chose your own path, and I, in my stubbornness, let it be. I should have stood by you, as a father should. Instead, I let pride and fear cloud my judgment. I did not wish to see you leave this place, and so I kept you from it. That was my mistake.

I have spent these years in silence, watching the sea from my tower, and in its endless rhythm I have found solace and sorrow. The lighthouse is my world now, and the light that guides the lost ships is the only thing that keeps me from despair. I

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with regret. It has been five long years since the last time we spoke, and now, as the storm gathers strength outside these cold, stone walls, I find myself at a crossroads. I fear I may not see the dawn.

You were never the daughter I wanted. I raised you with discipline, with expectations, with a love that was too often buried beneath the weight of duty. I told myself that I was doing what was right, that I was protecting you from a world that would only ever disappoint. But I was wrong, Eliza. I was wrong to think that love could be measured in rules and consequences. I was wrong to believe that you could be shaped into something that would make me proud.

I have spent years in this lighthouse, watching the sea, listening to the wind, and trying to find peace. But peace has always eluded me. I have never known how to be a father, and I have never known how to be a man. I have watched the light flicker and fade, just as I have watched my own life flicker and fade, caught between duty and desire.

I have thought of you often. I have thought of the way you laughed, the way you looked at me with eyes that saw beyond my stern face, and the way you once told me that I was the only one who could ever truly understand you. I have thought of

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

**April 12th, 1894**

I hope this letter finds you in good health, though I daresay it is more likely to find you in some state of melancholy, as I have been for the last five years, ever since you left this house and this life behind. I have not written to you, nor have you written to me, and yet I have never stopped thinking of you. Every night, as I stand on the cliff and watch the sea, I see your face in the waves, and I hear your voice in the wind. I do not know if it is madness or memory, but I cannot bring myself to let go of the hope that one day you will return.

It has been a terrible winter, and the winds have been like the cries of a child in distress. The lighthouse has stood for over thirty years, and though it has weathered many storms, I fear this one may be the last. The sea is restless, and the sky is dark with clouds that seem to weep. I have heard the old sailors say that this storm will be like the one that swallowed the *Eleanor*, the ship that vanished without a trace in 1889. I have no wish to be remembered as the man who was swallowed by the sea, but I am not so foolish as to believe I can outlast it.

I have been keeping the light burning all night, though

**April 3rd, 1889**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside these ancient walls. The sea roars with a fury that has not been seen in decades, and I fear the storm may take more than just the light from my tower. I have kept this light burning for thirty years, and now, as the night deepens and the heavens weep, I find myself questioning whether I shall see the dawn.

Five years have passed since you left, and five years have not changed the ache in my chest. I have not spoken to you, nor have you written. I have heard nothing from your home in London, not even a letter. You were so certain that I had no place in your life, that I was but a relic of the past, a man who had long since lost his place in the world. But I have not lost my place, not in the world, and not in my heart.

I have kept the lighthouse, as you would have said, "in a state of decay." The lantern is dim, the oil is thin, and the light is but a whisper against the storm. Yet I have not let it die. I have tended it with the care of a man who knows that even in the darkest night, there is a need for light.

I have thought of you often, Eleanor. I have watched the waves crash against the

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write to you now with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five long years have passed since we last spoke, and I find myself wondering if you still remember the sound of my voice, or if the memories have faded like the paint on the old lighthouse walls.

The sea has been restless these past few nights, and I fear the storm that approaches may be the end of me. I have spent my life watching the waves, knowing when they would rise and when they would fall, but never have I felt such an unrelenting fury in their wake. The wind howls like a beast, and the rain lashes against the windows with a violence I have never known.

I have not written to you in so long because I did not know what words could say. I thought, perhaps, that time would mend the rift between us. But time, my dear, is a cruel thing. It does not heal wounds; it merely covers them with dust and memory.

You were a child when you left, and I was a man who had long since stopped believing in the power of love. I told myself that you needed to go, that the world beyond this lonely shore held more for you than the life I could offer. But I was wrong, Eleanor. I was wrong to let you go, and I have spent every day since wondering if I could have done more, if I could have been more.

I have seen the light from the

My Dearest Eliza,

I write to you now with the weight of the storm pressing down on the cliffs like the hand of God Himself. The wind howls through the tower as though it seeks to tear it from its foundations, and the sea, that ancient and unrelenting force, roars with a fury I’ve never known. It is as if the very world is trying to reclaim what it once had, and I fear I may not be strong enough to hold it back.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years of silence, of unanswered letters, of nights where I would sit by the light and wonder if you had ever even read one. I know now that you did not. Not because you were cruel, but because you were lost. I have no right to judge you, but I do not understand you. You were my daughter, my light in the darkness, and now you are a shadow I cannot reach.

I have always believed that a lighthouse keeper must be steady, unshaken by the tempests of the world. That is what I have tried to be—steadfast, even when the sea rose in wrath, even when the world felt cold and indifferent. But tonight, I am not sure I can be. The sea roars in defiance, and I fear it may be the last time I speak to you. But if the storm does not claim me, I must say these words, for they have been weighing upon me like the stones of this very tower.

I do not know if you still remember the sound of my voice, or if the memory of your father has faded like the last embers of a dying fire. But I hope, in the deepest part of your soul, you still remember the man I once was—before the sea took everything.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

**From Your Father, Thomas Hargrave, Keeper of the Lighthouse at Blackmoor Point**

**April 12th, 1889**

I write to you now with a quill that trembles in my hand, for the wind howls like a banshee and the sea roars as if it would swallow the very rocks upon which I stand. Yet, I must write, for I fear I may not live to see the dawn, and if I do, I would not leave this world without a word from your father.

Five years have passed since you left this house, Eliza. Five years since you closed the door behind you, as if you were closing the door on your father’s heart. I have not heard from you, nor have I seen your face in the streets of London, nor heard your voice in the church or in the market. You have become a ghost, a memory, a shadow of what you once were.

I know the world has changed, and perhaps you have changed too. But I do not believe you have changed for the better. You were always too proud, too quick to judge, too quick to walk away. I have always told you that pride is a dangerous companion, and I fear you have taken that lesson to heart.
